title: Ben “Emilio” Ojemann
text:
Hi! My name is Ben Ojemann, though I also go by Emilio. I am currently a double major in Hydrologic Sciences & Policy and Physical Geography at UC Santa Barbara.
I grew up in Seattle, WA where the Pacific Northwest instilled in me a deep love for the great outdoors and the environment. After moving away for undergrad at UCSB, I have committed myself to improving natural resources through rigorous coursework, field work, and research.

title: About Me
text:
Where I’m From
I was born in the beautiful city of Mazatenango, Guatemala before permanently relocating to Seattle, Washington.
 
Growing up in Seattle largely shaped who I am today. I was lucky to have been surrounded by the towering Cascades mountains, powerful rivers, lush forests, and strong coastlines. I was instantly drawn the outdoors. This connection is a big reason why I ended up studying the environment.



What I’m doing at UCSB
I am currently working towards two degrees at UC Santa Barbara: one in Hydrology and the other in Physical Geography. My academic path has been shaped by an obsession with water and the diverse California landscapes. On the hydrology side, I’ve worked through coursework covering ground and surface water systems, soil science and policy frameworks governing the management of these resources. On the geography side, I’ve completed two full GIS series where I built technical skills in spatial analysis, cartography, and remote sensing in ArcGIS, Google Earth Engine, and Python.
Beyond coursework, I am a Student Research Lead at the university’s Cheadle Center for Biodiversity and Ecological Restoration, where I conduct ecological research, work with interns, and contribute to wetland restoration at the North Campus Open Space (NCOS).
 


Hobbies
When I’m not in the field or staring at satellite imagery, you’ll probably find me on a nearby field, playing ultimate frisbee. I’ve played competitively since middle school, and it’s one of my favorite ways to relieve stress, connect with people, and occasionally hit the grass for a layout!

title: About Me
text:
I was born in the beautiful city of Mazatenango, Guatemala before relocating to Seattle, Washington.
 Growing up in Seattle largely shaped who I am today. I was lucky to have been surrounded by the towering Cascades mountains, powerful rivers, lush forests, and strong coastlines. I was instantly drawn the outdoors. This connection is a big reason why I ended up studying the environment.

title: Visualizing Waterbird-Aquatic Invertebrates Linkages at NCOS
text:
Overview
The North Campus Open Space (NCOS) is an actively restoring estuary situated directly adjacent to the UC Santa Barbara campus. As a Student Research Lead at the Cheadle Center for Biodiversity and Ecological Restoration, I designed and led my own study investigating the spatial and trophic relationships between local waterbirds and aquatic invertebrates across four habitat zones in the upper arms of the Devereux Slough.



Photograph: Bill Dewey




The Question
As NCOS continues to restore its historic tidal connectivity and diverse, native habitats, a key ecological management question emerges: how are waterbirds and their invertebrate prey distributed across the wetland, and how do habitat zones influence those patterns? Getting a good understanding of these these linkages can directly inform restoration decisions such as deciding to prioritize habitat work


Methods
To investigate these patterns, I combined monthly waterbird survey data with quarterly aquatic invertebrate sampling across four distinct habitat zones at Devereux Slough: the main channel and its mudflats, the Phelps Creek confluence, constructed freshwater ponds, and seasonal vernal pools. Each zone was ecologically distinct (different salinity gradients, water depths, and vegetation structures) despite being a part of the same restoration site. After linking the invertebrate community in each zone with published waterbird diets, I built trophic networks: visual maps of who is eating what, and where. All spatial analysis and network visualization were carried out in ArcGIS and R.


Results
The main channel and mudflats support the greatest waterbird diversity at NCOS, with a dense, generalist-dominated network of 25 waterbird species feeding on 9 invertebrate taxa. The Phelps Creek confluence tells a different story: Rails and Willets specialize heavily on oligochaeta worms. This saline-freshwater junction may allow for reduced competition between specialicists and generalists as it creates such a unique niche. Overall, there is high overlap of waterbird species overlap across all four zones, with only the Wilson’s Snipe recorded exclusively in vernal pools.


Takeaways

Food web structure, not just water presence, largely shapes how waterbirds use restored wetland habitats.
Fine-scale habitat monitoring captures ecologically meaningful variation that broad site-level surveys miss entirely - something restoration managers at other natural reserves can directly work on.

View full poster on eScholarship
I presented my work at the UCSB Undergraduate Research and Creative Activities (URCA) poster colloquium on May 13, 2026.

title: Visualizing Waterbird-Aquatic Invertebrates Linkages at NCOS
text:
The North Campus Open Space (NCOS) is an actively restoring wetland adjacent to UC Santa Barbara’s campus. As a Student Research Lead at the Cheadle Center for Biodiversity and Ecological Restoration, I designed and led a study investigating the spatial and trophic relationships between waterbirds, aquatic invertebrates, and tidal dynamics across four habitat zones at Devereux Slough.



Photograph: Bill Dewey

title: Visualizing Waterbird-Aquatic Invertebrates Linkages at NCOS
text:
The Question
As NCOS continues to restore tidal connectivity and native habitat, a key management question emerges: how are waterbirds and their invertebrate prey distributed across the wetland, and how does tidal stage influence those patterns? Understanding these linkages can directly inform restoration decisions — where to prioritize habitat work, and what ecological benchmarks to track over time.

title: Visualizing Waterbird-Aquatic Invertebrates Linkages at NCOS
text:
Methods
I conducted systematic waterbird point-count surveys across four habitat zones while simultaneously collecting aquatic invertebrate samples. I then built bipartite trophic networks linking bird species to invertebrate prey based on diet literature, and analyzed how network structure varied by habitat and tidal stage using GIS-based spatial analysis in ArcGIS and R.

title: Visualizing Waterbird-Aquatic Invertebrates Linkages at NCOS
text:
Results
This work was presented at the UCSB Undergraduate Research and Creative Activities (URCA) poster colloquium in May 2026.
View full poster on eScholarship

title: Visualizing Waterbird-Aquatic Invertebrates Linkages at NCOS
text:
What We Found
The slough supports the greatest waterbird diversity on site, with a dense, generalist-dominated network connecting 25 waterbird species to nine invertebrate taxa. The Phelps Creek confluence tells a different story — rails and Willets there specialize heavily on Oligochaeta worms, likely because reduced competition with generalists at this unique saline-freshwater junction opens up that niche. Species overlap across all four zones is high, with only the Wilson’s Snipe recorded exclusively in vernal pools.
The big takeaway: food web structure, not just water presence, shapes how waterbirds use restored wetland habitat. Fine-scale habitat monitoring captures ecologically meaningful variation that broad site-level surveys miss entirely — something restoration managers at NCOS can directly act on.
I presented my work at the UCSB Undergraduate Research and Creative Activities (URCA) poster colloquium in May 2026.
